course_info.title: International Law
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 1 hour per session

class_schedule.topic: Sources and Nature of International Law
learning_objectives: Identify the sources of international law: treaties, custom, general principles, judicial decisions
Explain the relationship between international law and sovereignty
Debate whether international law is really 'law' without a central enforcer

lecture_notes.introduction: International law governs relations between states and increasingly affects individuals. But in a world of sovereign states, how can law exist without a world government?
key_concepts: Sources per ICJ Statute Art. 38: treaties, customary law, general principles, judicial decisions, scholarly writings
Treaty law: Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties (1969)
Customary international law: state practice + opinio juris
Jus cogens: peremptory norms permitting no derogation
The compliance puzzle: why states obey international law
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the Nicaragua v. United States (1986) case to identify how the ICJ applied different sources of law

review.value: Law arising from consistent state practice accompanied by a belief that the practice is legally required (opinio juris), binding on all states
Peremptory norms of international law from which no derogation is permitted, including prohibitions on genocide, slavery, torture, and aggression

learning_objectives: Define statehood under the Montevideo Convention criteria
Explain the principles of sovereign immunity and non-intervention
Analyze jurisdictional claims: territorial, nationality, universal
readings: Montevideo Convention on the Rights and Duties of States (1933)

lecture_notes.introduction: Sovereignty is the foundational concept of international law. It defines who the subjects are, what rights they hold, and the limits of jurisdiction.
key_concepts: Montevideo criteria: territory, population, government, capacity for relations
Recognition: declaratory vs. constitutive theories
Sovereign immunity: absolute vs. restrictive doctrines
Jurisdiction: territorial, nationality, protective, passive personality, universal
Self-determination: internal vs. external, remedial secession
lecture_notes.demonstration: Debate: does Taiwan meet the Montevideo criteria for statehood?

review.value: Statehood requires a permanent population, defined territory, effective government, and capacity to enter into relations with other states
The principle that any state may prosecute certain crimes (genocide, war crimes, crimes against humanity) regardless of where they occurred or the nationality of the perpetrator

learning_objectives: Explain the treaty-making process: negotiation, signature, ratification, entry into force
Analyze the principles of pacta sunt servanda and rebus sic stantibus
Evaluate reservations, amendments, and treaty withdrawal
readings: Vienna Convention on the Law of Treaties (selected articles)

lecture_notes.introduction: Treaties are the primary source of international law. Understanding how they are made, interpreted, and terminated is essential legal literacy.
key_concepts: Treaty formation: negotiation, adoption, signature, ratification
Pacta sunt servanda: agreements must be kept in good faith
Reservations: opt-outs from specific treaty provisions
Treaty interpretation: text, context, object and purpose (VCLT Art. 31-32)
Treaty termination: withdrawal, material breach, rebus sic stantibus
lecture_notes.demonstration: Drafting exercise: simulated treaty clause with reservations and interpretive declarations

review.value: The fundamental principle that treaties are binding on the parties and must be performed in good faith (VCLT Article 26)
The doctrine that a fundamental change of circumstances not foreseen by the parties may be invoked as grounds for terminating or suspending a treaty

learning_objectives: Trace the development of the international human rights framework
Analyze the International Bill of Human Rights and major conventions
Evaluate enforcement mechanisms and their limitations

lecture_notes.introduction: Human rights law has transformed international law by making individuals, not just states, subjects of legal protection. But enforcement remains the central challenge.
key_concepts: Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948): aspirational foundation
ICCPR and ICESCR: binding covenants on civil/political and economic/social rights
Regional systems: European, Inter-American, African human rights courts
Treaty body monitoring and the Universal Periodic Review
Cultural relativism vs. universalism in human rights
lecture_notes.demonstration: Moot court exercise: a human rights complaint before the UN Human Rights Committee

review.value: The UDHR, ICCPR, and ICESCR together form the foundation of international human rights law, covering civil, political, economic, social, and cultural rights
The argument that human rights standards should be interpreted in light of local cultural contexts, challenged by universalists who insist on non-derogable core rights

class_schedule.topic: International Humanitarian Law and the Law of Armed Conflict
learning_objectives: Distinguish between jus ad bellum and jus in bello
Apply the Geneva Conventions' protections for civilians and combatants
Analyze the legality of specific military actions under IHL

lecture_notes.introduction: Even in war, there are rules. International humanitarian law seeks to limit suffering by protecting those who do not participate in hostilities and restricting the means and methods of warfare.
key_concepts: Jus ad bellum: when states may lawfully use force (UN Charter Art. 2(4), 51)
Jus in bello: how force may be used (distinction, proportionality, necessity)
Geneva Conventions (1949) and Additional Protocols
Protection of civilians, POWs, and the wounded
War crimes, crimes against humanity, and individual criminal responsibility
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case analysis: evaluating a military strike against a dual-use target under IHL principles

review.value: The IHL requirement to distinguish between combatants and civilians at all times; deliberately targeting civilians is a war crime
An attack is unlawful if the expected civilian harm is excessive in relation to the concrete and direct military advantage anticipated

learning_objectives: Explain the jurisdiction and operation of the International Criminal Court
Analyze the challenges of international criminal accountability
Evaluate alternatives to criminal prosecution: truth commissions, transitional justice

lecture_notes.introduction: From Nuremberg to the ICC, international criminal law represents the most ambitious attempt to hold individuals accountable for the gravest crimes under international law.
key_concepts: Nuremberg and Tokyo tribunals: establishing precedent
Ad hoc tribunals: ICTY and ICTR
The ICC: Rome Statute, complementarity principle, jurisdiction
Challenges: state non-cooperation, selectivity, victor's justice
Transitional justice: truth commissions, reparations, institutional reform
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulated ICC preliminary examination: assessing whether a situation meets the threshold for investigation

review.value: The ICC acts only when national courts are unwilling or genuinely unable to investigate and prosecute crimes within their jurisdiction
The range of processes and mechanisms for dealing with a legacy of large-scale past abuses, including trials, truth commissions, reparations, and institutional reform

quizzes.topic: Sources of International Law and Treaty Law

questions.question: What are the two elements required to establish customary international law, and why is each necessary? Provide an example of a customary international law norm.
questions.answer: Customary international law requires (1) consistent state practice -- widespread and representative behavior by states over time, and (2) opinio juris -- the belief that this practice is legally required rather than merely habitual or customary. Both elements are necessary because practice alone could be mere coincidence or courtesy, while belief alone without actual practice would be aspirational rather than law. An example is the prohibition on torture, which has become customary law through widespread state practice of prohibiting it in domestic law and the near-universal belief that it is legally forbidden, even by states that do not always comply.

questions.question: Explain the principle of pacta sunt servanda and the exception of rebus sic stantibus. Under what conditions might a state invoke rebus sic stantibus to withdraw from a treaty?
questions.answer: Pacta sunt servanda (VCLT Art. 26) means treaties are binding and must be performed in good faith. Rebus sic stantibus (VCLT Art. 62) provides a narrow exception: a state may invoke a fundamental change of circumstances to terminate or withdraw from a treaty if (1) the circumstances at the time of conclusion were an essential basis of consent, (2) the change was unforeseen, and (3) the change fundamentally transforms the obligations still to be performed. This is interpreted very restrictively to prevent abuse; states cannot invoke minor or foreseeable changes.

quizzes.topic: Human Rights Law and International Humanitarian Law

questions.question: Distinguish between jus ad bellum and jus in bello. Explain why a state can fight a lawful war (just cause) but still commit war crimes, and vice versa.
questions.answer: Jus ad bellum governs when states may lawfully resort to force (self-defense under UN Charter Art. 51, or Security Council authorization). Jus in bello governs how force is used during armed conflict (distinction, proportionality, necessity under the Geneva Conventions). These are independent bodies of law: a state fighting in self-defense (lawful jus ad bellum) can still violate jus in bello by targeting civilians. Conversely, an aggressor state (unlawful jus ad bellum) whose soldiers scrupulously follow IHL rules does not commit war crimes. The legality of the war and the legality of conduct within it are assessed separately.

questions.question: What is the principle of distinction in international humanitarian law? Why is targeting a hospital being used as a military command center a difficult legal question?
questions.answer: The principle of distinction requires parties to an armed conflict to distinguish at all times between combatants and civilians, and between military objectives and civilian objects. Only military objectives may be targeted. A hospital used as a military command center is difficult because it involves a dual-use object: it retains its protected civilian status under IHL (Geneva Convention IV), but its use for military purposes can cause it to lose that protection. However, even then, the attack must satisfy proportionality (civilian harm must not be excessive relative to military advantage) and precautionary measures must be taken (warning, alternative means). The legal analysis requires weighing military necessity against humanitarian considerations in each specific case.

major_assignments.title: International Law Case Brief and Analysis
major_assignments.description: Select a landmark International Court of Justice or International Criminal Court case. Produce a comprehensive legal brief and a critical analysis evaluating the court's reasoning and its implications for international law.
objectives: Demonstrate legal research and briefing skills
Analyze judicial reasoning in international law
Evaluate the impact of a case on international legal norms
Apply legal principles to a specific factual context
deliverables: Structured case brief (facts, issue, holding, reasoning, significance)

criteria: Accurate statement of facts and legal issues
Correct identification of applicable legal principles
Thorough analysis of the court's reasoning
Proper use of legal terminology and citation
Identifies strengths and weaknesses of the ruling
Considers dissenting and separate opinions
Evaluates broader implications for international law
Engages with scholarly commentary on the case
Primary sources (case text, treaties) used correctly
Scholarly secondary sources engaged
Proper legal citation format
Comprehensive annotated source list
Clear and persuasive oral argument
Structured presentation of legal reasoning
Effective response to questions and challenges
Professional demeanor
Rigorous legal briefing and analysis of a landmark international case
Critical evaluation of judicial reasoning and its implications
Strong legal research with proper citation
Effective oral advocacy and presentation

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on legal principles, cases, and treaty provisions

moot_court_participation.description: Active participation in moot court exercises and legal debates

major_assignments.description: Case brief, analysis paper, and oral argument

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering sources of law, sovereignty, treaties, and human rights

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam including hypothetical legal problem analysis

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for moot court sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences from moot sessions results in zero participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late briefs are penalized 10% per day. No extensions within 48 hours of deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Moot court preparation may be collaborative. All written briefs and analysis must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Proper legal citation is required. Plagiarism or fabrication of legal authorities results in course failure and referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to international law databases (Westlaw International, HeinOnline), ICJ and ICC case repositories.
